source: CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
title: Ensure alerts are defined on custom views to notify of unauthorized requests, critical account actions, and high-impact operations in your account

input_row.source: CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
input_row.title: Ensure alerts are defined on custom views to notify of unauthorized requests, critical account actions, and high-impact operations in your account
input_row.description: Events that you collect and centralize in the IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service provide information about actions that take place on your account. You can define alerts to notify promptly of problems, anomalies, and abnormal situations.
input_row.rationale: IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA adds audit capabilities to your IBM Cloud architecture. Each event reports information about who requested an action on your account (initiator details), what action was requested, the resource on which the action was requested (target details), and outcome of the request. Each event is based on the Cloud Auditing Data Federation (CADF) standard. For more information about the IBM event definition, see Event types and Event fields. Events from enabled IBM Cloud services and core IBM Cloud platform services are collected automatically and available for analysis through the audit instances in your account. Check out the services that send events to the platform service events instance. Core platform Activity Tracker events are available for all IBM Cloud services. These events include provisioning and deletion of service instances, tagging, and IAM management. By using the live streaming tail feature through the Everything view , you can diagnose issues, analyze stack traces and exceptions, identify the source of errors, and monitor different event sources through a single view. By using custom views, you can show a subset of data by applying pre-defined filters and natural language queries to the Everything view . On custom views, you can issue alerts to be notified of important actions. To act promptly on events that you identify as critical or warning, DevOps teams can configure alert notification integrations to the following systems: email, Slack, web hook, and PagerDuty. Impact: The recommended alerts on custom views allow you to monitor actions that take place in your account, the level of threat of those actions in your account, and abnormal situations. If you are missing any of these views and the ability to check that events are available through them, exposes a potential security risk by hiding a possible gap of events that are critical to control what happens in the account. In addition, views can have alerts attached to them so that you are notified quickly about abnormal situations. If you do not have the right views, you lose the ability to configure alerts and pre-empt situations that might require your attention.
input_row.audit: Use the following checklist to ensure that events are collected and processed to identify anomalies or abnormal events. This checklist assumes that you have a different IBM Cloud account for development, staging, Q&A, and production. The checklist must be applied to each environment independently. 1. Check that alerts are defined based on templates, known as presets in LogDNA. When you use a preset, you can manage access control to notifications from a centralize location. The alert condition should indicate the following information: o Type of alert: presence o Condition: Notify when 1 or more matches appear within 1 minute o Send the alert when 1 line shows in the view For example, when you configure an email alert, using a preset helps you manage access control to alert notifications. When a user configures an email alert directly on a view, you specify directly on the view’s alert who the recipients are. If you decide to remove a user, you must manually delete that user from the alert. If you have multiple views, you must check view by view that the user is not a recipient of the email alert. 2. For each custom view, check the purpose of the view and what notification channels are configured. o Email alerts: Check that an email alert is used to verify that the alert on a view is working, and to inform users of situations about which they have requested information. o Slack alerts: Check that a Slack alert is used to inform users about routine scenarios that they might want to monitor. o PagerDuty alert: Check that a PagerDuty alert is used to notify users immediately so that they can take prompt action. o Webhook alerts: Check that a webhook alert is used when users want to use a third-party tool to get and manage notifications. 3. Check that alerts have been configured for each of the following custom views: o Custom views that are defined for each service to monitor events that have an outcome of failure. o Custom views that are defined for each service to monitor events that have a severity set to critical. These events report deletion actions in the account. o Custom views that are defined for service to monitor events that have a severity set to warning. These events report actions that modify the state of a resource in your account. o Custom views that are defined for each service to monitor events that have a reason code set to 403 or 409. These events report requests on a service that are not authorized. o Custom views that are defined for each service to monitor events that report actions on access groups, changes of policies, user management, service IDs. These events report IAM actions in the account. o Custom views that report Security Advisor findings, that is, security incidents that are monitored by IBM Cloud Security Advisor in your account. UI instructions that you can use to check that alerts on custom views are defined and working. This checklist assumes that you have a different IBM Cloud account for development, staging, Q&A, and production. The checklist must be applied to each environment independently. 1. Log in to IBM Cloud at https://cloud.ibm.com. 2. Go to the Menu icon. Then, select Observability to access the Observability dashboard. 3. Click Activity Tracker . 4. For each region where you run operations, launch the Activity Tracker in that region by clicking View LogDNA . Then, complete the following steps. You must always check Frankfurt because this region is where platform information is available. 5. Check that alert definitions and notification channels are defined: o Select Settings . Then, select Alerts . o Check that alerts are defined based on templates, known as presets in LogDNA. o In the View-specific alerts section, check for individual alerts. If you have individual alerts, identify the type (email, Slack, webhook, PagerDuty), the LogDNA view that is associated with the alert, and notification destination. Check that there is a business need for the alert. o In the Presets section, check that each notification channel is correct and has valid destinations. Then, check that the views that are included in each preset send alerts to the destinations that are configured in the preset. 6. Check that all views outlined in the previous control have at least one alert notification channel configured: o Select Views . o For each view, select the view name. Then, select Edit alert . o Select each notification channel, and verify the condition: ▪ Type of alert: presence ▪ Condition: Notify when 1 or more matches appear within 1 minute ▪ Send the alert when 1 line shows in the view o For each notification channel, verify the purpose of the alert and the configuration details: ▪ Email alerts: Check that an email alert is used to verify that the alert on a view is working, and to inform users about situations that they have requested information about. Check that the recipient is a functional ID. If individual users are included in the recipients, check that they have clearance to access the information. ▪ Slack alerts: Check that a Slack alert is used to inform users about routine scenarios that they might want to monitor. Check that the webhook corresponds to a channel and the people in the channel have clearance to access the information. ▪ PagerDuty alert: Check that a PagerDuty alert is used to notify users immediately so that they can take prompt action. Check that the PagerDuty (PD) credentials correspond to the PD instance where you are managing alerts. ▪ Webhook alerts: Check that a webhook alert is used when you want to use a third-party tool to get and manage notifications. Check the webhook configuration is valid and goes to the third- party tool. 7. For each custom view and alert notification channel, verify that you see an alert notification so that you can validate that the alert is being generated.
input_row.remediation: UI instructions that you can use to create alerts on custom views: 1. Log in to IBM Cloud athttps://cloud.ibm.com. 2. Click the Menu icon. 3. Click Observability > Activity Tracker . 4. For each region where you run operations, launch the Activity Tracker in that region by clicking View LogDNA . Then, complete the following steps. You must always check Frankfurt because this region is where platform information is available. 5. Configure alerts on views for each audit instance. Default Value: IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA does not include default templates for views or alerts. You can define your own views, alerts, and notification channels. Default notification channels are configured by using presets. References: 1. IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA: Adoption guidelines for regulated and highly available workloads: https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/Activity-Tracker-with- LogDNA?topic=logdnaat-adoption 2. IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service plans: https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/Activity-Tracker-with-LogDNA?topic=logdnaat- service_plan
input_row.impact: The recommended alerts on custom views allow you to monitor actions that take place in your account, the level of threat of those actions in your account, and abnormal situations. If you are missing any of these views and the ability to check that events are available through them, exposes a potential security risk by hiding a possible gap of events that are critical to control what happens in the account. In addition, views can have alerts attached to them so that you are notified quickly about abnormal situations. If you do not have the right views, you lose the ability to configure alerts and pre-empt situations that might require your attention.

IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA: Adoption guidelines for regulated and highly available workloads: https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/Activity-Tracker-with- LogDNA?topic=logdnaat-adoption 2. IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service plans: https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/Activity-Tracker-with-LogDNA?topic=logdnaat- service_plan

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**

This control involves checking the specific configurations of alerts on custom views within the IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA service. The audit process requires verification of the alert definitions, notification channels, and the specific types of events being monitored through custom views. Although these configurations may be accessible in part via IBM Cloud APIs or console operations, the complexity and specificity of the checks, such as verifying the purpose and correctness of each notification channel and the business logic behind alert definitions, suggest a need for human judgment and visual inspection. As a result, these intricacies are best suited for a manual review to ensure all configuration details align with organizational requirements.

**MANUAL STEPS:**

1. **Log in to IBM Cloud:**
   - Access the IBM Cloud console at https://cloud.ibm.com.
   
2. **Access Activity Tracker:**
   - Navigate to the Menu icon, select "Observability," and then click on "Activity Tracker."

3. **Region-specific Operations:**
   - For each region where operations are running, click "View LogDNA" to access the Activity Tracker in that region, ensuring you include Frankfurt for platform-wide information.

4. **Verify Alerts Configuration:**
   - Select "Settings," then "Alerts." 
   - Confirm alerts are defined based on LogDNA templates (presets). Check types (email, Slack, webhook, PagerDuty) and the corresponding view associations and destinations.

5. **Check Custom View Alerts:**
   - Navigate to "Views." For each custom view, select the view name and then "Edit alert."
   - Verify notification channels and alert conditions: Type of alert (presence), condition (Notify when 1 or more matches appear within 1 minute).

6. **Review Notification Channels:**
   - Examine each notification channel (email, Slack, PagerDuty, webhook) for correct configuration, ensuring the recipient list includes only authorized personnel with proper clearance.
   
7. **Confirm Business Logic:**
   - Evaluate the alerts against organizational needs and security policies, ensuring that all necessary alerts are configured correctly and serve their intended purpose effectively.

This manual process ensures that all alerts and views are configured and operational as required, examining the alignment with the business's security objectives and regulatory demands.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Manual is partially justified. While the manual inspection is necessary for verifying the alignment of alert configurations with organizational requirements and business logic, many aspects of this control can be automated using IBM Cloud APIs and CLI. For instance, IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA provides APIs that can be used to list alert configurations and notification channels, which could automate the verification of their presence and basic configuration details. However, the evaluation of business logic and alignment with security policies would still require manual inspection.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 6

- The manual steps provided are generally accurate for a visual inspection of alert configurations within the IBM Cloud console. However, the assessment lacks mention of specific IBM Cloud CLI commands or API methods that could automate parts of this process. For example, using the IBM Cloud CLI with LogDNA-specific commands or APIs to list and describe alert configurations could streamline parts of the audit. The program naming convention is not applicable here as no specific script or program is referenced.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Automation Potential:** Incorporate IBM Cloud CLI commands or API calls to list alert configurations and notification channels. For example, using `ibmcloud logging alert-list` or similar commands to retrieve alert information.
  
- **Program Naming:** If developing a script to automate parts of this process, consider naming it `ibm_activity_tracker_alerts_configuration_check` to clearly indicate its purpose.

- **Missing Considerations:** The assessment should consider the use of IBM Cloud Schematics or Terraform for managing infrastructure as code, which could help in maintaining consistent alert configurations across environments.

- **Alternative Methods:** Utilize IBM Cloud SDKs to programmatically access and verify alert configurations, which could be integrated into a CI/CD pipeline for continuous compliance monitoring.

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- The assessment provides a reasonable approach but lacks integration of available automation tools that could enhance efficiency and accuracy. There is room for improvement by leveraging IBM Cloud's capabilities more effectively.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Modify:** Integrate IBM Cloud CLI and API capabilities to automate the retrieval and basic verification of alert configurations and notification channels. Retain manual inspection for evaluating business logic and alignment with security policies. This hybrid approach will optimize the audit process by combining automation with necessary manual checks.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Manual

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
While IBM Cloud Activity Tracker with LogDNA provides APIs and CLI commands to list alert configurations and notification channels, the control requires verification that alerts are defined on custom views specifically to notify of unauthorized requests, critical account actions, and high-impact operations. This necessitates human judgment to interpret the intent and appropriateness of each alert, which cannot be fully automated. Automation can confirm the presence of alerts, but it cannot assess whether the alerts are correctly configured to meet the nuanced requirements of this control.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. Log in to the IBM Cloud Console at https://cloud.ibm.com.
2. Navigate to the Menu icon → Observability → Activity Tracker.
3. For each region where your account operates (including Frankfurt for platform events), click "View LogDNA" to access the Activity Tracker instance.
4. In each Activity Tracker instance, go to "Settings" → "Alerts."
5. Review the list of defined alerts:
   - Confirm that alerts exist for custom views monitoring unauthorized requests, critical account actions, and high-impact operations.
   - Verify that each alert is based on appropriate LogDNA templates (presets) and is mapped to the correct notification channels (e.g., email, Slack, webhook, PagerDuty).
   - Ensure the alert conditions (filters, thresholds) align with the intent of detecting unauthorized or critical actions.
6. Document evidence (screenshots or exported configurations) showing that the alerts meet the control requirements.
7. Repeat for each environment (development, staging, QA, production) as required by the control.

**WHY NOT AUTOMATED:**  
Although IBM Cloud APIs and CLI can list alert configurations, they cannot interpret whether the alert logic and custom views are correctly designed to detect unauthorized, critical, or high-impact actions as required by the control. This evaluation requires human understanding of business context and security policy alignment, which is not possible to automate reliably.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
The first AI recommended a manual approach due to the need for human judgment. The second AI suggested partial automation but acknowledged that full compliance verification (intent and appropriateness of alerts) requires manual review. I resolved this by prioritizing the need for human interpretation of alert logic and mapping, which cannot be automated, thus selecting a manual approach.

**SOURCE:** CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
